era_context: The early 770s CE are characterized by Charlemagne's rise to sole power in the Frankish Kingdom, the consolidation of the Abbasid Caliphate, the Tang Dynasty's recovery from the An Lushan Rebellion, and the Byzantine iconoclastic controversy. This is an era of significant political transitions, military campaigns, and religious conflict across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records; archaeological dating imprecise)

events.title: Charlemagne becomes sole ruler of the Frankish Kingdom
events.description: Following the death of his brother Carloman in 771, Charlemagne becomes the sole ruler of the Frankish Kingdom. Charlemagne had previously ruled the western portion of the kingdom (Austrasia) while Carloman ruled the eastern portion (Neustria). The death of Carloman and Charlemagne's subsequent consolidation of power marks a turning point in Frankish history and sets the stage for Charlemagne's unprecedented expansion and the creation of the Carolingian Empire. This succession is attested in the Royal Frankish Annals.
events.certainty_note: Carloman's death in 771 and Charlemagne's consolidation of power are well-documented in contemporary Frankish sources. The date is certain.
cross_references: 768-001 (Charlemagne and Carloman inherit the Frankish Kingdom)

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate: Consolidation of administrative authority
events.description: Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) continues the consolidation of Abbasid administrative authority and the suppression of internal revolts. The year 771 falls within the mature phase of al-Mansur's reign, marked by the stabilization of the Abbasid state and the establishment of a centralized bureaucracy. Al-Mansur's administrative innovations lay the groundwork for the Abbasid golden age and the flourishing of Islamic civilization.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Al-Mansur's reign and administrative activities are well-attested, but specific events in 771 are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Daizong's reign continues
events.description: Emperor Daizong (r. 762–779 CE) continues his reign during the recovery phase following the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE). The year 771 falls within Daizong's reign, a period marked by efforts to restore central authority, manage provincial military governors, and recover economically from the rebellion. Daizong's reign is characterized by both achievements in stabilization and challenges from powerful military commanders.
events.certainty_note: Daizong's reign (762–779) is well-documented in Chinese imperial records. The year 771 falls securely within his reign.

events.title: Byzantine Empire: Constantine V's iconoclastic policies
events.description: Emperor Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) continues his enforcement of iconoclasm in the Byzantine Empire. The year 771 falls within the height of Constantine V's iconoclastic campaign, which had begun in 741. This policy remains deeply controversial and divisive within Byzantine society and the church. Constantine V's iconoclasm is one of the most significant religious controversies of the medieval Christian world.
events.certainty_note: Constantine V's reign and iconoclastic policies are well-documented. The year 771 falls securely within his reign.

events.title: Uighur Khaganate: Consolidation of Central Asian power
events.description: The Uighur Khaganate continues to consolidate its control over Central Asia and the Silk Road trade routes. The year 771 falls within a period of Uighur expansion and increasing influence in Central Asian politics. The Uighurs would become major allies and rivals of the Tang Dynasty throughout the late 8th century, playing a crucial role in the balance of power in Central Asia.
key_figures: Uighur khans (names not consistently preserved in contemporary sources)
events.certainty_note: The general consolidation of Uighur power in the 770s is attested, but specific events in 771 are not clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Charlemagne's consolidation of power in 771 is sometimes overstated in popular historiography. While Carloman's death was important, Charlemagne's rise to dominance was a gradual process that continued through the 770s and 780s. The conquest of Lombardy (773–774) and the Saxon Wars (772–804) were equally or more significant in establishing Charlemagne's power. Modern scholarship emphasizes that Charlemagne's empire was built through sustained military campaigns rather than a single succession event. The dating of specific Abbasid, Tang, and Uighur events to 771 is often uncertain; sources provide general patterns rather than precise annual records.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 771 CE is strong for Western Europe (Frankish sources), moderate for the Byzantine Empire and Tang China, and sparse for Central Asia and other regions. Charlemagne's consolidation of power is well-documented in the Royal Frankish Annals. However, sources for Scandinavia, Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania are absent. The surviving sources reflect the interests of literate elites and likely underrepresent economic, social, and cultural history. The dating of some events shows uncertainty across sources, particularly for Central Asian and Uighur events.

graph_edges.note: Charlemagne's consolidation of power in 771 enables his invasion of Lombardy in 773
Charlemagne's consolidation of power in 771 is the foundation for his imperial coronation in 800
Charlemagne's consolidation of power in 771 enables his Saxon campaigns beginning in 772
